topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the office was a physical weight pressing down on Elias. He kept running a hand over the smooth, cool surface of his desk, the friction a small, desperate attempt to ground himself. The framed photograph of his mentor, Dr. Alistair Finch, sat accusingly on the corner of the blotter. Alistair was gone. Gone, as if the air had simply evacuated the room. Elias had been waiting for a final word, a casual nod, anything to acknowledge the seismic shift in their working relationship, but the reception had been a dead end. He picked up the phone, his thumb hovering over Alistair’s number, then let it drop onto the receiver with a soft, defeated clatter. The sheer emptiness of the absence made his chest feel tight, like a band squeezing too tightly.

[story 2]

Clara paced the length of her apartment three times before stopping abruptly, her breath catching in short, sharp gasps. The neatly organized bookshelves seemed to mock her; every volume stood sentinel, untouched, while the life force of the space had visibly drained away. She re-read the email, the words swimming before her eyes, each sentence echoing hollowly. It was just a notification, a formality, devoid of any warmth, no farewell gesture. Clara clenched her jaw so hard she tasted copper. She walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights, her shoulders hunching inward as if trying to shrink away from the vastness of the unanswered departure.

[story 3]

Mark stared at the empty chair across the conference table, the polished wood reflecting a distorted image of his own anxious face. He kept fiddling with the edge of his tie, the silk feeling abrasive against his skin. Every minute detail of the departure—the lack of a planned meeting, the curt administrative note—was being replayed in a loop behind his eyes, sharp and relentless. He leaned forward, resting his elbows on the table, his knuckles white where he gripped the edge. A low, involuntary sound escaped his throat, a ragged exhale that held all the unresolved tension coiled within him. He didn't move, just held the tension in place, waiting for a reaction that wouldn't come.
